elements.sent-2411-001: It is shown that the sum set $K+K'$ has equal box and Hausdorff dimensions and in this number named $s$, $H^s(K+K')<\infty$.
elements.sent-2411-002: Moreover, for almost every pair $(K,K')$ satisfying $HD(K)+HD(K')\leq 1$, there is a dense subset $D\subset \mathbb R$ such that $H^s(K+\lambda K')=0$, for all $\lambda\in D$.
elements.sent-2411-003: It also is shown that in the context of affine Cantor sets with two increasing maps, there are generically (topological and almost everywhere) five possible structures for their sum: a Cantor set, an L, R, M-Cantorval or a finite union of closed intervals.
elements.sent-2411-004: Keywords: Hausdorff dimension, arithmetic sum of Cantor sets, Palis' conjecture.
elements.sent-2411-006: Affine Cantor sets have been regarded, on one hand as a natural generalization of the homogeneous Cantor sets and on the other hand as a particular case of regular Cantor sets.
elements.sent-2411-007: An affine Cantor set can be obtained via an iteration process on the finite union of many discrete closed intervals not necessarily same lengths.
elements.sent-2411-008: In dynamical systems theory, regular Cantor sets are defined as repellers of smooth expanding maps.
elements.sent-2411-009: They also may appear from intersections of hyperbolic sets with stable or unstable manifolds of its points.
elements.sent-2411-010: The arithmetic difference of two regular Cantor sets plays a key role in the study of homoclinic bifurcations \cite{PT .}
elements.sent-2411-011: In geometric measure theory, regular Cantor sets may be defined as the attractors of the iterated function systems (IFSs) whose satisfy the open set condition.
elements.sent-2411-012: Assume that affine Cantor sets $K$ and $K'$ are the attractors of the IFSs $\{r_ix+t_i\}_{i=1 ^{M}$ and $\{r_j'x+{t' _j\}_{j=1 ^{N}$ with convex hulls $[0,~a]$ and $[0,~b]$, respectively.}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Throughout the paper, abbreviation $BD(A),~HD(A),~H^s(A)$, $|A|$ and $A^\circ $ denote the Box dimension, Hausdorff dimension, $s$-dimensional Hausdorff measure, Lebesgue measure and interior of $A$, respectively \cite{Fa1 .}
elements.sent-2411-014: Moreira \cite{M2 proved that if $K$ and $K'$ are two regular Cantor sets, then \begin{equation}HD(K-K')=\min\{HD(K)+HD(K'), 1\},\end{equation} provided that one of Cantor sets is non essentially affine.}
elements.sent-2411-015: The set of all resulting pairs forms a general subset (open and dense subset) of the space of all pairs of $C^2$-regular Cantor sets.
elements.sent-2411-017: Peres and Shmerkin \cite{PS proved (1.1) provided that $\frac{\log r_i { \log r_j'}\notin \mathbb{Q}$ for at least a pair $(i,j)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-018: They also introduced some examples to show that in cases where $\frac{\log r_i { \log r_j'}\in \mathbb{Q}$ for all $(i,j)$, dimension drop may occur.}
elements.sent-2411-019: Our first result is as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-020: For given affine Cantor sets $K$ and $K'$, $BD(K-K')=HD(K-K'):=s$ and $H^s(K-K')<\infty$.
elements.sent-2411-021: Also, if $HD(K)+HD(K')\leq 1$ and $\frac{\log r_i { \log r_j'}\notin \mathbb{Q}$ for at least a pair $(i,j)$, then there exists a dense subset $ D\subset \mathbb{R}$ such that $H^s(K-\lambda K')=0$ for all $\lambda \in D$.}
